Here's what I have on file right now, under `~/.agents/homes/chief-of-staff/workflows/`:

**1. Bug report triage** — you flag a bug informally (symptom/project, not a repo/file). I identify the right repo, check tenex-edge for prior context, search existing issues to avoid dupes, then file an evidence-backed GitHub issue + a durable record in `everything/investigations/` (and a decision entry if judgment is needed).

**2. Feature request triage** — you describe a feature loosely (example commands/JSON, not a literal spec). I ground it in the *actual* repo code first (not just docs/commits), then file a precise issue on the repo that owns it, plus a note in `projects/<slug>/`. I don't implement unless asked.

**3. Decisions kanban** — anything that needs your judgment goes on [Project 5, "Needs Pablo" column](https://github.com/users/pablof7z/projects/5) as a GitHub issue in `pablof7z/everything`. You comment to answer; I act on it and close/move it. You only watch that one column.

**4. Ops board** — broader in-progress-work board (same Project 5): Status (Pablo's Inbox / In Progress / Blocked / Done), Type, Owner, Repository. You only watch the "Pablo's Inbox" view; everything else is passive visibility I maintain.

**5. Inbox monitor** — a standing launchd background loop (`inbox-watch-loop.sh`) polling Project 5's "Pablo's Inbox" every ~8s, detecting new comments from you, and dispatching a headless Claude session to act on them automatically.

**6. Knowledge capture** — when you hand over material (launch docs, positioning, domain knowledge, "here's how X works"), I file it as `projects/<slug>/kb/<category>/<entry>.md` in `everything`, additive, same-session — not left to live only in chat.

If what's on your mind doesn't fit one of these, I'll treat it as `unknown-task`: do it directly if it's simple/reversible, ask only if it changes priority/authority/money/irreversible state, and capture a new workflow once the shape is clear.

Done — pushed to the same PR: **https://github.com/pablof7z/touch-grass/pull/2**

Your idea is now the architecture. Here's what it does and why it's a good move:

## The entrypoint: `scripts/session_start.py`

The agent runs **one script** at session start instead of following a static instruction. The script decides what to inject:

- **Home not tracked yet** (plain dir, not a symlink) → injects `references/SETUP.md`, a runbook that walks the agent through identifying/creating the tracking repo, migrating home contents into `<repo>/.agents/homes/chief-of-staff/`, and symlinking back. It also appends a live listing of what's currently in the home dir so the agent knows exactly what to migrate.
- **Home tracked** → injects the session brief: tracked git location, the workflow list (reusing `workflows.py`), and an optional `BRIEF.md`.

## Why this gives you the flexibility you wanted

- **Self-evolution / onboarding** — the setup path is self-healing: a fresh machine or fresh install auto-guides itself through repo bootstrap, no human memory required.
- **Proactive tracking + cronjobs** — `BRIEF.md` in the home dir is a standing note the agent (or you) writes to, and it resurfaces *every* session automatically. That's where "inbox-monitor loop is alive," "3 decisions waiting on Pablo," etc. live. In my test the brief surfaced exactly that.
- **Grow via the script, not the prompt** — new proactive sections (daily-report pointer, heartbeat checks, stale-blocker scan) get added in `build_brief()`, so context stays lean instead of bloating agent.yaml every turn. That's consistent with the constraint you set last turn.

The single agent.yaml change is a net simplification: "list workflows at session start" → "run `session_start.py` and follow what it injects." That one *is* standing doctrine (it happens every session), so it belongs there.

Tested all three cases (fresh / existing-content / tracked); all render correctly. Decisions are logged in `docs/product/chief-of-staff.md`.
